agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand signals. The CEO's description is intentionally high-level and leaves significant ambiguity around guardrails, thresholds, and operational constraints.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing / Rate Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "Undefined aggregate signal representing customer request volume — exact measurement method unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — thresholds unspecified" },
      { "term": "Real-time", "definition": "Prices adjust continuously or near-continuously in response to demand changes — latency tolerance unspecified" },
      { "term": "Fully automated", "definition": "No manual human intervention required for price adjustments under normal operation" },
      { "term": "Competitive", "definition": "Prices must remain attractive relative to market alternatives — no benchmark defined" },
      { "term": "Profitable", "definition": "Prices must cover costs and contribute margin — minimum margin unspecified" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must adjust in real time in response to demand changes",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a product capability, not a data integrity constraint. The system could temporarily fall back to static pricing without corrupting state.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand level changes, a new price is computed and made available within the real-time SLA window (undefined — see escalation)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "When demand increases, price must increase relative to the baseline price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is core business policy — a directional pricing rule. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during certain demand surges (e.g., natural disasters).",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand metric D2 > D1, the computed price P2 >= P1 (monotonically non-decreasing with demand)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "When demand decreases, price must decrease relative to the baseline price",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Symmetric directional policy. Could be overridden — e.g., prices may have a sticky floor to protect margin.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand metric D2 < D1, the computed price P2 <= P1 (monotonically non-increasing as demand falls)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Price must never fall below cost (profitability constraint)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Selling delivery below cost violates fundamental business viability. If this is violated, the system is actively destroying value with every transaction. This is a financial integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every computed price P and delivery cost C: P > C (or P >= C + minimum_margin if margin floor is defined)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price must not exceed an upper guardrail (price ceiling)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The ceiling value is a policy decision — it could change quarterly, by region, or be overridden for premium tiers. But the existence of SOME ceiling is strongly implied by 'prices don't go crazy.'",
      "testable_assertion": "For every computed price P and configured ceiling MAX: P <= MAX"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Price must not fall below a lower guardrail (price floor)",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The floor value is a policy decision, distinct from BR-4's cost constraint. The floor may be set above cost to protect brand positioning.",
      "testable_assertion": "For every computed price P and configured floor MIN: P >= MIN"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual human approval required per transaction",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a design goal, not a data integrity constraint. Manual override capability is likely needed for incident response, regulatory compliance, or edge cases — the CEO just doesn't want manual as the default mode.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price computation and application occur without human intervention in the critical path under normal operating conditions"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The price presented to a customer at booking time must be the price they are charged",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an implicit but fundamental invariant of any real-time pricing system. If the price changes between quote and charge, you have a bait-and-switch — a legal and trust violation. The system state is corrupt if quoted price != charged price.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a customer is quoted price P at time T and confirms booking, the charged amount equals P regardless of demand changes after T"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "Demand must be measured by a defined, consistent metric before it can drive pricing",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If the demand signal is undefined or inconsistent, every price output is meaningless — the system is producing arbitrary numbers, not demand-driven prices. This is an input integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every price computation must reference a quantifiable demand metric that is consistently defined and measurable"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "Price changes must be auditable — every adjustment must be traceable to the demand signal that caused it",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "For a fully automated system adjusting prices in real time, auditability is a regulatory and operational integrity requirement. Without it, you cannot debug, comply with pricing regulations, or defend against price-gouging claims.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every price change event records: timestamp, previous price, new price, demand metric value, and the rule/algorithm that produced the change"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., sudden weather event)", "expected_behavior": "Price increases but is capped by the upper guardrail; system must not overshoot or oscillate" },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price falls to the floor guardrail, never below cost; system must not produce nonsensical prices" },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request a quote simultaneously during a price transition", "expected_behavior": "Each customer gets a consistent quote; race conditions must not result in one customer being charged a stale or inconsistent price" },
    { "scenario": "Cost of delivery changes while dynamic pricing is active", "expected_behavior": "The profitability invariant (BR-4) must be re-evaluated against the new cost basis; price floor may need to adjust upward" },
    { "scenario": "System outage or demand signal unavailability", "expected_behavior": "Undefined — must be specified. Likely fallback to last known good price or static baseline, but CEO did not address this" },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price-gouging laws triggered during emergency demand surge", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails must account for legal price ceilings that may be lower than business-configured maximums — not addressed in spec" },
    { "scenario": "Competitor drops price to zero during a price war", "expected_behavior": "'Competitive' goal conflicts with 'profitable' goal — which takes priority? Not defined" },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly (flapping)", "expected_behavior": "Price should not oscillate at the same frequency — some dampening or smoothing is needed to avoid customer confusion and billing chaos" }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can increase revenue 10-25% in high-demand periods but risks customer churn if perceived as unfair. Below-cost pricing (BR-4 violation) would cause direct financial loss on every transaction.",
    "operational": "Fully automated pricing removes manual rate-setting burden but introduces operational complexity: monitoring, incident response for pricing anomalies, guardrail tuning, demand signal reliability.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which may erode trust and predictability. Transparency of pricing logic is critical for retention. Surge pricing in ride-sharing has proven controversial — delivery is analogous.",
    "risk": "HIGH. Price-gouging regulations vary by jurisdiction and could expose the company to legal liability. Reputational risk from perceived unfair pricing. Technical risk from pricing algorithm errors causing mass under/over-charging.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Pay variable prices — may feel gouged during high demand or get deals during low demand. Trust and fairness perception is critical." },
      { "group": "Finance / Revenue team", "impact": "Revenue becomes less predictable; need new forecasting models. Margin protection depends on guardrail configuration." },
      { "group": "Operations / Dispatch", "impact": "Demand signals must be reliable and real-time; operations must feed accurate data to pricing." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance", "impact": "Must review pricing against price-gouging statutes, consumer protection laws, and contractual obligations with enterprise clients." },
      { "group": "Sales / Account Management", "impact": "Enterprise clients with negotiated rates may be excluded or need override logic. Dynamic pricing may conflict with contractual SLAs." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability; need training and tools to explain pricing decisions." },
      { "group": "Couriers / Drivers", "impact": "If higher prices don't translate to higher courier pay, retention risk. If they do, margin impact must be modeled." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is an elevator pitch, not a specification. It establishes directional intent (demand-driven pricing, automation, guardrails) but defines zero concrete thresholds, no demand measurement methodology, no guardrail values, no fallback behavior, no regulatory considerations, and no stakeholder-specific carve-outs. BR-4 (profitability) and BR-8 (quote integrity) are high-confidence invariants I can assert from general business principles, but almost everything else requires follow-up. This is a 30% confidence spec.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Price must increase with demand (directional intent is explicit)",
      "Price must decrease with lower demand (directional intent is explicit)",
      "Some form of guardrails will exist (CEO explicitly stated this)",
      "System will be automated (CEO explicitly stated this)",
      "Price must not go below cost (fundamental business principle)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "What exactly 'demand' means — request volume? Courier availability? Geographic density? Time-of-day?",
      "What the guardrail values are — no floor or ceiling specified",
      "What 'real-time' means — sub-second? Per-minute? Per-hour?",
      "What 'competitive' means — against whom? Measured how?",
      "Fallback behavior when demand signal is unavailable",
      "Whether enterprise/contract customers are subject to dynamic pricing",
      "Whether price-gouging regulations have been considered",
      "Whether courier compensation is affected by dynamic pricing",
      "The pricing algorithm or function shape (linear? exponential? tiered?)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "This is for B2C delivery pricing, not B2B contract rates",
      "Demand is measured at some geographic and temporal granularity (not globally)",
      "Guardrails include both a floor and a ceiling",
      "Quote-to-charge price consistency is required (standard commerce invariant)",
      "Auditability is required for any automated pricing system (regulatory baseline)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Demand' is never defined. Is it order volume per hour? Courier supply vs. request ratio? Geographic clustering? The entire pricing model depends on this definition and it is completely absent.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Data Science", "Operations", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Define demand as order volume per zone per hour", "Define demand as courier-to-order ratio", "Define demand as a composite index of multiple signals"],
      "recommendation": "Require Product to define the demand metric formally before any implementation begins. This is the single most critical missing piece."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Guardrails so prices don't go crazy' — no concrete values, no specification of whether this means absolute bounds, percentage-based bounds, rate-of-change limits, or some combination.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Legal", "Customers", "Product"],
      "options": ["Absolute price ceiling/floor in dollars", "Percentage multiplier cap (e.g., max 2x base price)", "Rate-of-change limit (e.g., max 10% change per 5 minutes)", "All of the above"],
      "recommendation": "Define guardrails as both absolute bounds AND rate-of-change limits. Absolute bounds protect against extreme prices; rate-of-change limits prevent jarring customer experience."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory compliance. Many jurisdictions have price-gouging laws that cap price increases during emergencies. A fully automated system that ignores these could create legal liability.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal", "Compliance", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["Add jurisdiction-aware price caps", "Disable dynamic pricing during declared emergencies", "Set guardrails conservatively enough to always comply"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review is mandatory before launch. Recommend jurisdiction-aware guardrails that automatically tighten during declared emergencies."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Competitive' and 'profitable' are stated as co-goals but can directly conflict. When competitors undercut aggressively, which goal wins?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Executive team", "Finance", "Sales"],
      "options": ["Profitability always wins (floor is floor)", "Competitiveness wins within a loss budget", "Different rules for different customer segments"],
      "recommendation": "Profitability must be the hard constraint; competitiveness is the optimization objective within that constraint. Selling below cost to match competitors is a strategic decision requiring executive approval, not an automated rule."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of how dynamic pricing interacts with enterprise/contract customers who may have negotiated fixed rates or rate cards.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Sales", "Account Management", "Enterprise customers"],
      "options": ["Exclude contract customers entirely", "Apply dynamic pricing but honor contractual ceilings", "Renegotiate contracts to include dynamic pricing clauses"],
      "recommendation": "Exclude contract customers from DynaPricing in v1 and handle as a separate workstream with Sales/Legal."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No specification of fallback behavior when the system fails — demand signal unavailable, algorithm error, or system outage. For a fully automated system, this is a critical operational gap.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Operations", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Fall back to last known good price", "Fall back to static base price", "Halt orders until pricing is restored"],
      "recommendation": "Fall back to static base price with an alert to operations. Never halt orders due to pricing system failure."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
